facts.correctAnswer: December 7, 1941
facts.quizQuestion: On what date did Japan launch its surprise attack on Pearl Harbor, drawing the United States into World War II?
facts.explanation: December 7, 1941 — FDR called it 'a date which will live in infamy.' The attack unified an isolationist American public overnight, turning divided opinion into near-unanimous support for war.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of battleships burning in Pearl Harbor at dawn, smoke columns rising against an orange sky, Japanese planes in formation overhead

facts.quizQuestion: How many Americans were killed in the Japanese attack on Pearl Harbor on December 7, 1941?
facts.explanation: 2,403 Americans died — 1,177 of them aboard the USS Arizona alone when her forward magazine exploded. The Arizona still rests on the harbor floor; oil still seeps from her hull, which sailors call 'the tears of the Arizona.'
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the USS Arizona battleship engulfed in a massive explosion and fire, debris flying, small rescue boats in the foreground

variants.quizQuestion: Which US warship suffered the single greatest loss of life at Pearl Harbor, with 1,177 killed?

variants.correctAnswer: True — Pearl Harbor killed 2,403 vs approximately 360 at Midway

variants.quizQuestion: Of the 2,403 Americans killed at Pearl Harbor, ____ died on the USS Arizona alone.

facts.correctAnswer: They were at sea on missions and escaped the attack
facts.quizQuestion: Why did Japan's attack on Pearl Harbor fail to destroy the US aircraft carriers, which would prove decisive in the Pacific war?
facts.explanation: Japan's planners designed the attack to cripple battleships — the traditional symbol of naval power. By 1942, carriers had replaced battleships as the decisive weapon. The USS Enterprise, Lexington, and Saratoga were all at sea that day, a stroke of luck that changed the war's outcome.

Japan chose not to target carriers to avoid provoking a full response
US carriers had all been transferred to the Atlantic Fleet months earlier
Carriers were docked at a separate classified facility outside Pearl Harbor
Japan's bombers ran out of fuel before reaching the carrier moorings
US code-breakers warned carriers to leave the night before
A second Japanese strike wave was cancelled before it could find them
The carriers were scuttled by their crews to prevent capture

variants.quizQuestion: Which class of warship did Japan mistakenly prioritize over aircraft carriers at Pearl Harbor, focusing on an already-obsolete weapon?
variants.correctAnswer: Battleships
distractors: Destroyers

facts.quizQuestion: How many Japanese aircraft carriers did the United States sink at the Battle of Midway in June 1942?
facts.explanation: All four Japanese carriers — Akagi, Kaga, Soryu, and Hiryu — were sunk in one day of combat. Japan had used those same four carriers at Pearl Harbor. Their loss took years to replace, and the experienced pilots aboard could never be replaced at all.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a Japanese aircraft carrier in flames and listing heavily to one side, a US dive bomber pulling out of its attack run above, smoke filling the sky

variants.correctAnswer: False — the US sank 4 Japanese carriers; Japan sank 1 US carrier (Yorktown)

facts.correctAnswer: US codebreakers had cracked Japanese naval codes and knew the attack plan in advance
facts.quizQuestion: What gave the US the decisive intelligence advantage at the Battle of Midway that turned an ambush into a catastrophe for Japan?
facts.explanation: Station HYPO at Pearl Harbor, led by Commander Joseph Rochefort, broke Japan's JN-25 naval code. Rochefort's team even used a clever deception — broadcasting a fake water shortage at Midway to confirm the target Japan called 'AF.' Admiral Nimitz knew the attack was coming before Japan's fleet left port.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a dimly lit signals intelligence room, rows of analysts at desks covered in coded papers, a single overhead light, US Navy pennant on the wall
distractors: A Japanese officer defected to the US with battle plans
US radar stations detected the Japanese fleet three days out
A neutral merchant ship spotted and reported Japan's fleet
Japan accidentally broadcast its battle orders in plain language
British intelligence intercepted a diplomatic cable revealing the target
A captured Japanese submarine yielded navigation charts
US spy planes photographed Japan's carrier formation before the battle
Australia's coast watchers tracked Japan's fleet through island chains

variants.quizQuestion: Commander Joseph Rochefort led the intelligence unit that cracked Japan's naval codes before Midway. Where was his unit based?
variants.correctAnswer: Station HYPO at Pearl Harbor
distractors: Washington DC's Op-20-G
Corregidor in the Philippines
Brisbane, Australia
San Francisco naval base

variants.quizQuestion: US codebreakers suspected Japan's Midway target was codenamed 'AF' but needed confirmation. How did they cleverly verify this?
variants.correctAnswer: They broadcast a fake water shortage at Midway and intercepted Japan's coded reply confirming 'AF' had a water problem
distractors: They sent a fake defector to Japanese naval headquarters
They found 'AF' labeled on a captured Japanese map

variants.correctAnswer: True — Japanese overconfidence in their codes was a fatal error
distractors: False — Japan changed codes two weeks before Midway but the US adapted quickly
False — Japan suspected a leak but blamed it on ship sightings, not codebreaking

variants.correctAnswer: JN-25

facts.correctAnswer: 3,500 experienced Japanese naval aviators
facts.quizQuestion: Beyond the four carriers, what irreplaceable loss at Midway truly crippled Japan's ability to wage offensive naval air warfare?
facts.explanation: Japan lost approximately 3,500 highly trained naval aviators at Midway — men who had taken years to train and had combat experience from China, Pearl Harbor, and across the Pacific. Japan's training pipeline couldn't replace them. By 1944, Japan was sending barely-trained pilots to their deaths in kamikaze attacks.

5,000 ground troops
200 carrier officers
1,800 mechanics and ground crew
4,500 infantrymen

variants.correctAnswer: Kamikaze suicide attacks using poorly trained pilots
distractors: Drafting civilian merchant sailors into combat roles
Purchasing fighter aircraft from Germany
Abandoning carrier operations entirely in favor of battleships
Relying on land-based aircraft for all naval air support

variants.correctAnswer: Training a skilled naval aviator took years, and the pipeline couldn't produce replacements fast enough
distractors: Japan had a small population and had already mobilized all eligible men

variants.correctAnswer: False — carriers could be built, but experienced pilots took years to train and couldn't be replaced quickly
distractors: True — carriers were Japan's primary strategic asset and their loss was irreplaceable

facts.quizQuestion: How far were American and Filipino prisoners of war forced to march during the Bataan Death March in April 1942?
facts.explanation: 80,000 prisoners — starving, sick, and exhausted from months of combat — were marched over 60 miles in brutal tropical heat. Guards bayoneted those who fell. Around 11,000 died on the march or shortly after arrival in prison camps, making it one of the worst atrocities committed against US forces.

facts.quizQuestion: From which US aircraft carrier did Lieutenant Colonel Doolittle's 16 B-25 bombers launch their surprise raid on Tokyo in April 1942?
facts.explanation: B-25 Mitchell bombers were never designed to fly from a carrier — their wingspan barely cleared the deck. The Hornet sailed to within 650 miles of Japan. The raid caused minimal physical damage but was a massive psychological shock: Japan's civilians had been told their homeland was impregnable.

facts.correctAnswer: August 7, 1942 to February 9, 1943 (approximately 6 months)
facts.quizQuestion: How long did the grueling Allied campaign for Guadalcanal last, making it one of the longest and most costly battles of the Pacific war?
facts.explanation: Six months of jungle warfare, naval battles, and tropical disease. Marines who landed expecting a quick victory faced relentless Japanese counterattacks, food shortages, and outbreaks of malaria that put half the force out of action. Guadalcanal proved the Pacific would be won yard by bloody yard.

facts.correctAnswer: Capture strategically vital islands and bypass heavily fortified ones, leaving them to wither
facts.quizQuestion: What was the core principle of the US 'island-hopping' strategy in the Pacific from 1943-1945?
facts.explanation: Admiral Nimitz and General MacArthur developed complementary versions of this approach. Rather than assaulting every Japanese stronghold, the Allies captured key islands for airfields and bases, then leapt ahead — leaving massive Japanese garrisons like Rabaul with 100,000+ troops stranded, cut off, and irrelevant to the war's outcome.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a top-down strategic map of the Pacific Ocean with island groups marked, arrows jumping between them in a hopping pattern toward Japan, some islands marked with red X symbols to show they were bypassed
distractors: Capture every Japanese-held island in sequence from east to west
Use amphibious landings to push Japan back island by island in a straight line
Concentrate all forces on the nearest islands to Japan for a rapid advance
Establish a naval blockade around each island before invading
Use Marine divisions to assault every fortified Japanese position directly
Divide forces to simultaneously attack multiple islands in parallel
Lure Japanese fleets to decisive naval engagements near each island
Neutralize each island with air power before any ground forces landed

variants.quizQuestion: Island-hopping bypassed massive Japanese bases like Rabaul. What eventually happened to the 100,000+ Japanese troops stranded there?
variants.correctAnswer: They were cut off from supply and reinforcement and played no further role in the war
distractors: They were rescued by submarine and redeployed to defend the Philippines
Allied forces eventually had to assault Rabaul at great cost
They surrendered in 1943 when food supplies ran out

variants.correctAnswer: Airfields for land-based bombers (especially B-29s)
distractors: Submarine pens to interdict Japan's shipping lanes

variants.correctAnswer: True — bypassing strongholds saved lives compared to a sequential island-by-island assault
distractors: False — the strategy accepted higher casualties in exchange for faster progress

variants.correctAnswer: Airfields

facts.correctAnswer: Divine wind
facts.quizQuestion: What does 'kamikaze' mean in Japanese — the name given to suicide pilots who crashed their planes into Allied ships from 1944-1945?
facts.explanation: The name references a legendary typhoon that scattered Mongol invasion fleets in the 13th century, saving Japan from conquest. Japan invoked the same divine protection in 1944 — framing suicide pilots as the nation's last mystical defense. The reality was industrial: Japan was running out of experienced pilots and quality aircraft.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a Japanese Zero fighter plane diving steeply toward a US battleship, trailing smoke, the rising sun insignia visible on its wings, desperate anti-aircraft fire rising from the ship below

Sacred warrior
Spirit sacrifice
Heaven's arrow
Emperor's fist
Iron typhoon
Noble death
Wind of war

variants.quizQuestion: The kamikaze name references a historical event that supposedly saved Japan from conquest in the 13th century. What was that event?
variants.correctAnswer: Typhoons that destroyed Mongol invasion fleets attempting to land in Japan
distractors: A volcanic eruption that sank Chinese warships near Kyushu
A fierce samurai counterattack that drove off Korean invaders
A storm that wrecked a Portuguese fleet threatening Japanese trade routes
A legendary naval victory over a Chinese emperor's armada

variants.quizQuestion: Kamikazes sank 34 ships and damaged 368 more. What made them particularly difficult for Allied ships to defend against?
variants.correctAnswer: A plane under human control could maneuver until the last second, making anti-aircraft targeting extremely difficult
distractors: Kamikazes flew in formations too large for anti-aircraft guns to cover

True — the waiting list to become a kamikaze was longer than the number of available planes

variants.quizQuestion: Kamikaze attacks killed approximately ____ Allied sailors — more than any other Japanese weapon in the war's final year.

facts.explanation: Joe Rosenthal's photograph of six Marines raising the flag on Mount Suribachi became one of the most reproduced images in history and was used to inspire the 7th War Bond drive. What few knew: this was the SECOND flag raised that day — a larger replacement flag. The first flag-raising had already been photographed, less dramatically.

facts.explanation: 7,000 Americans killed and 21,000 Japanese killed on a tiny volcanic island. Admiral Nimitz said 'Uncommon valor was a common virtue.' The battle's casualty rate alarmed US planners: if an 8-square-mile island cost this much, invading Japan's main islands — 145,000 square miles — would be catastrophic.

facts.correctAnswer: The largest amphibious assault in the Pacific Theater

facts.quizQuestion: What distinction does the Battle of Okinawa (April-June 1945) hold among all Pacific Theater battles of World War II?
facts.explanation: Over 180,000 US troops assaulted an island defended by 100,000+ Japanese soldiers and containing 500,000 civilians. The 82-day battle produced 50,000+ US killed and wounded. The sheer scale of suffering — including Okinawan civilians jumping from cliffs rather than surrender — directly influenced the decision to use atomic bombs instead of invading Japan.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of an enormous naval armada offshore — dozens of warships stretching to the horizon — while waves of US Marines and soldiers wade ashore on a long beach under smoke and shellfire
distractors: The first battle where kamikaze attacks were used against US forces

The only Pacific battle involving both US Army and Marine Corps divisions
The battle with the greatest concentration of anti-aircraft guns in the Pacific
The last battle before Japan's unconditional surrender

variants.quizQuestion: How many US troops were committed to the assault on Okinawa — the largest force assembled for any Pacific battle?
The Battle of Okinawa's staggering cost — 50,000+ US casualties in one battle — directly influenced a major strategic decision. What was it?
variants.correctAnswer: The decision to use atomic bombs on Japan rather than launch a ground invasion
distractors: The decision to demand unconditional surrender rather than negotiate terms
The decision to use firebombing against Japanese cities instead of precision raids

variants.quizQuestion: True or False: The civilian population of Okinawa was evacuated before the battle began.

distractors: True — Japan evacuated most civilians to Kyushu before the US assault
True — US leaflet drops convinced most civilians to flee to the northern mountains

facts.quizQuestion: How many kamikaze sorties did Japan fly during the battle of Okinawa alone — making it the most intense kamikaze campaign of the war?
facts.explanation: Okinawa saw the peak of kamikaze operations — over 3,000 sorties in Operation Kikusui ('floating chrysanthemums'). Japan launched massive coordinated waves. The US Navy suffered its worst losses of the entire war during this campaign, with over 400 ships damaged. Yet Japan couldn't stop the invasion — only delay and bleed it.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a US destroyer surrounded by explosions and smoke, multiple Japanese planes diving at it from different angles, crew manning anti-aircraft guns desperately, the chaos of a full kamikaze wave attack

facts.correctAnswer: 80,000 American and Filipino prisoners of war
facts.quizQuestion: How many prisoners of war were forced on the Bataan Death March in April 1942 — the largest surrender in US military history?
facts.explanation: 80,000 men — starving and sick after months of siege — surrendered when the Philippines fell. The sheer scale of the surrender shocked Americans. For the survivors who reached the prison camps, a worse ordeal was just beginning: years of captivity with starvation rations, forced labor, and brutal guards.

facts.correctAnswer: All planes ran out of fuel; crews bailed out over China or crash-landed; some were captured by Japan and executed
acceptableAlternatives: planes ran out of fuel over China
facts.quizQuestion: What happened to the 16 B-25 crews after they bombed Tokyo in the Doolittle Raid — none of them ever returned to their carrier?
facts.explanation: The plan was to land in Nationalist China, but early launch (spotted by a Japanese picket boat) meant they ran short of fuel. 15 planes crash-landed or crews bailed out over China; one landed in the Soviet Union and was interned. Eight airmen were captured by Japan — three were executed. Doolittle himself expected a court martial for losing all his planes; instead he received the Medal of Honor.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a B-25 bomber ditching into the dark sea at night near a rocky Chinese coastline, the crew climbing out of the sinking plane into small rubber rafts, no other aircraft visible

The planes were abandoned in the ocean and crews were rescued by submarine
Crews parachuted into the Yellow Sea and were picked up by US destroyers

facts.correctAnswer: First major US offensive of the Pacific war, proving Japan could be beaten on land
acceptableAlternatives: first Allied land victory over Japan
facts.quizQuestion: Why was the Allied victory at Guadalcanal in February 1943 considered a strategic turning point beyond its immediate tactical value?
facts.explanation: Before Guadalcanal, Japanese ground forces had not lost a major battle in the Pacific war. The island also validated the entire island-hopping concept — that the US could seize and hold enemy-held islands as stepping stones toward Japan. After Guadalcanal, the strategic initiative in the Pacific permanently shifted to the Allies.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of US Marines raising an American flag over a captured Japanese airfield in a jungle clearing, damaged Japanese Zero planes visible in the background, palm trees framing the scene
distractors: It was the first battle where US forces used radar to defeat a Japanese fleet

It proved the effectiveness of submarine warfare against Japanese supply lines
It was the first battle where US forces outnumbered Japanese defenders
It forced Japan to cancel its planned invasion of Australia
It demonstrated that American production could outpace Japanese manufacturing
It was the first time the US Army and Marine Corps fought side by side in the Pacific

facts.correctAnswer: Rabaul, with over 100,000 Japanese troops
facts.quizQuestion: Which massively fortified Japanese base — garrisoned by over 100,000 troops — did the Allies deliberately bypass under island-hopping strategy, leaving it stranded for the rest of the war?
facts.explanation: Rabaul on New Britain was Japan's primary South Pacific base, heavily defended with 100,000 troops, hundreds of aircraft, and massive coastal artillery. A direct assault would have cost tens of thousands of Allied lives. Instead, MacArthur and Nimitz simply bypassed it — isolating its garrison, cutting its supply lines, and making its enormous garrison irrelevant. By 1945, those 100,000 troops were growing vegetables to survive.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a top-down map view showing a large island base with airfields and harbor facilities, surrounded by a dotted bypass arrow showing Allied forces leaping past it to the next island chain
distractors: Truk Atoll
Peleliu Island
Corregidor
Tarawa Atoll
Wake Island
Kwajalein Atoll
Saipan
Bougainville

variants.quizQuestion: By 1945, what were Rabaul's stranded 100,000 Japanese troops doing to survive after being completely cut off from resupply?
variants.correctAnswer: Growing their own food — farming vegetables and raising livestock on the island
distractors: Launching nightly barge raids on nearby Allied-held islands for supplies
Surviving on stockpiled prewar supply depots the Allies had failed to destroy
Receiving supply drops from Japanese submarines that ran the Allied blockade
Fishing in the surrounding waters under air cover from hidden mountain airstrips

variants.quizQuestion: Bypassing Rabaul instead of assaulting it saved the Allies enormous casualties. What eventually happened to its 100,000 Japanese garrison?
variants.correctAnswer: They surrendered peacefully after Japan's August 1945 capitulation, never having fought

facts.correctAnswer: 34 ships sunk and 368 damaged
facts.quizQuestion: What was the total toll of Japanese kamikaze attacks on Allied ships across the entire 1944-1945 Pacific campaign?
facts.explanation: Despite 3,000+ sorties, kamikazes sank only 34 ships — mostly small destroyers and escort carriers — and damaged 368 more. The kill rate was lower than conventional attacks. Yet the psychological effect was enormous: sailors described the terror of watching a plane that refused to pull up. The US developed 'kamikaze picket' destroyers as a sacrificial warning screen.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a small US destroyer on fire amidships after a kamikaze hit, its bow and stern still above water, crew members in the water and on life rafts around it, two other warships rushing to assist
distractors: 150 ships sunk and 600 damaged
5 ships sunk and 50 damaged

20 ships sunk and 75 damaged

variants.quizQuestion: Why were kamikaze attacks particularly dangerous to destroyers assigned as radar picket ships near Okinawa?

distractors: Destroyers lacked radar-directed anti-aircraft guns unlike larger ships

variants.quizQuestion: True or False: Kamikaze pilots were more effective per sortie than conventional Japanese aircraft attacks.

facts.correctAnswer: Over 100,000 Okinawan civilians died — some jumping from cliffs rather than surrender
facts.quizQuestion: What was the tragic fate of Okinawa's civilian population during the battle, making Okinawa unique among Pacific battles for its civilian death toll?
facts.explanation: Over 100,000 Okinawan civilians died — roughly a quarter of the island's prewar population. Japanese propaganda had convinced many civilians that Americans would rape and murder them. Rather than surrender, groups of civilians leapt from cliffs at sites like Kyan and Itoman. The tragedy haunts Okinawa to this day and has fueled decades of anti-US base sentiment on the island.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Okinawan civilians sheltering in a coastal cave, terrified expressions, the sound of artillery audible, a narrow cave entrance with US soldiers visible outside — the impossible choice of surrender or death depicted
distractors: 500 civilians died; most were safely evacuated before the battle

200,000 civilians fled to Japan by boat during the battle
10,000 civilians were killed by US naval bombardment

30,000 civilians were killed by Japanese forces suspected of collaboration
75,000 civilians were taken prisoner and used as human shields

variants.quizQuestion: Japanese propaganda told Okinawan civilians Americans would harm them. What act of mass tragedy did this propaganda cause at sites like Kyan Cape?
variants.correctAnswer: Groups of civilians jumped from cliffs to their deaths rather than surrender to US forces
distractors: Civilians poisoned their water supplies to prevent US use

Civilian men killed their families and then themselves to avoid capture
Civilian groups attacked US positions with farm tools in suicidal charges

facts.correctAnswer: Japan underestimated American industrial capacity to replace losses and mobilize for war
facts.quizQuestion: Admiral Yamamoto, architect of the Pearl Harbor attack, privately feared the raid had made a catastrophic strategic error. What did he believe Japan had fatally underestimated?
facts.explanation: Yamamoto reportedly said: 'I fear all we have done is to awaken a sleeping giant and fill him with a terrible resolve.' Japan's planners believed the US, shocked by Pearl Harbor, would seek a negotiated peace. Instead, American factories converted to war production at a rate Japan couldn't match — building 90,000 aircraft in 1944 alone while Japan built 28,000.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a vast American factory floor at night, rows of half-built warplanes and tanks stretching to the horizon, hundreds of workers on multiple shifts, American flags hanging from the rafters
distractors: The fighting spirit of the US Marine Corps
The range and accuracy of American anti-aircraft guns
The speed at which US battleships could be repaired and returned to service
The loyalty and morale of Filipino troops fighting alongside Americans
The effectiveness of US submarine warfare against Japanese shipping
The diplomatic skill of American negotiators at the peace table
The accuracy of US naval gunnery at long range
The effectiveness of US Army Air Corps long-range bombers

variants.quizQuestion: In 1944, the US built roughly 90,000 military aircraft. How many did Japan build in the same year?
variants.correctAnswer: Approximately 28,000 — less than a third of US output
distractors: 85,000 — nearly matching US production
50,000 — about half as many

60,000 — two-thirds of US production

variants.quizQuestion: Japan's plan was to destroy the Pacific Fleet and negotiate peace before the US could rebuild. Why did this assumption prove fatally wrong?
variants.correctAnswer: Pearl Harbor united American public opinion, transforming isolationist sentiment into overwhelming support for total war
distractors: The US had secretly been planning war with Japan for years and had mobilization plans ready

variants.quizQuestion: True or False: Admiral Yamamoto, who planned the Pearl Harbor attack, was confident it would force the US into a peace settlement.
variants.correctAnswer: False — Yamamoto privately feared Japan had made a grave error and that American industrial might would eventually overwhelm Japan

facts.correctAnswer: Seven major naval battles were fought in the waters around Guadalcanal
acceptableAlternatives: naval battles in Iron Bottom Sound
facts.quizQuestion: The Guadalcanal campaign was not just a land battle — the waters around the island became a graveyard of ships. What earned the surrounding strait the grim nickname 'Iron Bottom Sound'?
facts.explanation: So many ships were sunk in the narrow strait between Guadalcanal and Savo Island that the channel was nicknamed Iron Bottom Sound. Both sides lost dozens of warships in seven major naval engagements. The US lost four heavy cruisers in a single night at the Battle of Savo Island — the worst US naval defeat of WWII. Japan's 'Tokyo Express' ran destroyers down 'The Slot' to resupply its garrison nightly.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a night naval battle in a narrow channel, muzzle flashes from battleships and cruisers illuminating dark water, a burning ship silhouetted against the flames, tropical jungle coastline visible on both sides
distractors: The shallow water made sonar useless for finding submarine wrecks

The rusting hulks of Japanese invasion barges covered the seabed
A single catastrophic storm sank an entire US destroyer squadron there
The area was named after the iron ore deposits in the surrounding hills
Japanese submarines sank so many Allied vessels that the floor was littered with wrecks
US carrier aircraft sank every ship Japan sent through the strait
The dark volcanic sediment of the channel bottom gave it an iron-colored appearance

variants.quizQuestion: Japan's nightly destroyer runs to resupply Guadalcanal were nicknamed the 'Tokyo Express.' What route did they use?
variants.correctAnswer: New Georgia Sound, nicknamed 'The Slot' — a natural channel through the Solomon Islands
distractors: The Lunga Roads — a sheltered anchorage near Henderson Field

distractors: True — Japan's superior night-fighting tactics gave it naval dominance throughout the campaign

variants.correctAnswer: Iron Bottom

facts.correctAnswer: Okinawa's catastrophic 50,000+ US casualties convinced planners an invasion of Japan would cost millions of lives
acceptableAlternatives: cost of invading Japan estimated from Okinawa
50000 US casualties influenced atomic bomb decision
facts.quizQuestion: How did the Battle of Okinawa directly influence President Truman's decision to use atomic bombs against Japan in August 1945?
facts.explanation: US planners used Okinawa as their model for estimating invasion casualties. Okinawa was 0.46% of Japan's land area — yet produced 50,000+ US casualties. Scaling up to Japan's main islands, military planners estimated between 250,000 and 1 million American deaths. Japanese estimates projected 20 million Japanese deaths in the defense. These projections made the atomic bomb — with its promise of ending the war without invasion — appear the lesser horror.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a war room with uniformed officers around a map table, casualty projection charts on the wall showing climbing numbers, a large map of Japan with invasion routes marked, grim expressions on every face
distractors: Okinawan civilians poisoned US water supplies, suggesting Japan would use chemical weapons in an invasion
Japan launched its first successful atomic bomb test during the Okinawa campaign
The Japanese garrison at Okinawa surrendered, suggesting the atomic bomb was unnecessary
Kamikaze attacks at Okinawa destroyed 50% of the invasion fleet, proving invasion was physically impossible
Soviet refusal to join the Pacific war forced the US to find an alternative way to end the conflict
Okinawa proved Japan had larger defensive forces than US intelligence estimated
Japanese civilians at Okinawa fought alongside soldiers, suggesting total civilian resistance to invasion
The Okinawa campaign took twice as long as planned, revealing intelligence failures about Japanese defenses

variants.quizQuestion: US military planners estimated a full invasion of Japan's main islands would cost how many American lives — based in part on Okinawa projections?
variants.correctAnswer: Between 250,000 and 1 million American deaths
distractors: 10,000-50,000 American deaths
50,000-100,000 American deaths
5 million American deaths
1,000-5,000 American deaths

variants.quizQuestion: Japanese planners had their own estimate for how many Japanese would die defending the home islands from a US invasion. What was that figure?

500,000 Japanese deaths
5 million Japanese deaths
100 million Japanese deaths

variants.quizQuestion: True or False: Okinawa was the only factor in the atomic bomb decision — historians agree there were no other significant motivations.
variants.correctAnswer: False — other factors included Soviet entry into the Pacific war, diplomatic goals, and preventing further conventional bombing deaths
distractors: True — declassified documents show Okinawa casualty projections were the sole driver of Truman's decision

variants.quizQuestion: Okinawa covered 0.46% of Japan's land area but produced 50,000+ US casualties. Planners ____ these numbers up to estimate invasion losses for Japan's main islands.

facts.correctAnswer: Carrier aircraft — opposing ships never saw each other; planes did all the fighting
facts.quizQuestion: What made the Battle of Midway historically unique as a naval battle — and what weapon system proved absolutely decisive?
facts.explanation: Midway was the first naval battle where the opposing fleets never saw each other — no battleship guns fired, no torpedoes ran between surface ships. Everything was decided by carrier aircraft flying hundreds of miles. The SBD Dauntless dive-bomber sank all four Japanese carriers in a few devastating minutes of attacks. The age of the battleship was definitively over.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of US SBD Dauntless dive-bombers plunging nearly vertical toward a Japanese aircraft carrier far below, anti-aircraft bursts surrounding them, the carrier's flight deck visible as a tiny rectangle from the bombers' perspective
distractors: Submarine torpedo attacks launched from below the surface
Battleship long-range gunfire at extreme range
Land-based bombers flying from Midway Island itself
Destroyer torpedo attacks at night under cover of darkness
Coordinated surface and air attacks from multiple directions simultaneously
Long-range cruise missiles guided by radio
Mines laid in advance across the anticipated Japanese route
Motor torpedo boats operating at high speed between the fleets

variants.quizQuestion: Which specific US aircraft type delivered the killing blows on all four Japanese carriers at Midway, attacking in steep dives?
variants.correctAnswer: SBD Dauntless dive-bombers
distractors: TBF Avenger torpedo bombers
F4F Wildcat fighters
B-17 Flying Fortress high-altitude bombers
PBY Catalina patrol planes

variants.quizQuestion: At Midway, US torpedo bombers attacked first and were almost entirely wiped out — but their sacrifice contributed to the later decisive dive-bomber success. How?
variants.correctAnswer: Torpedo attacks drew Japanese combat air patrol fighters down to sea level, leaving the dive-bombers' high-altitude approach uncontested
distractors: Torpedo bombers successfully damaged Japanese carrier flight decks, preventing aircraft launches
The torpedo attacks distracted Japanese damage control crews from spotting the incoming dive-bombers
Torpedo hits slowed two carriers enough for the dive-bombers to make accurate attacks
Torpedo bomber radio chatter misdirected Japanese anti-aircraft gun crews

variants.quizQuestion: True or False: The Battle of Midway proved that battleships, not aircraft carriers, remained the decisive weapon of naval warfare.
variants.correctAnswer: False — Midway proved the opposite: carrier aircraft were decisive and battleships played no role
distractors: True — Japan's battleships provided critical anti-aircraft fire that protected the carrier force
True — US battleships were the only ships that survived the battle unscathed

variants.quizQuestion: At Midway, the opposing US and Japanese surface fleets never came within ____ of each other — all combat was done by aircraft.
variants.correctAnswer: Visual range (hundreds of miles)
